58:{"role":"user","message":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"&lt;user_query&gt;\n1.  It's a reminder for going forward, often as you are working I can see in the agent window that you are having to make decisions about trying to figure out exactly what I mean.  I'ts often better for important forks in the road to just ask me.\n2.  Let's discuss a major issue I am hoping we can fix.  When you change .md files or .py files, the editor highlicghts in red code that will be deleted and highlights in green code that will be added.  Each of these changes asks for me to click approve or reject.  I LOVE THAT!!!  It is VERY IMPORTANT to me.  I downloadedand installed the cursor-notebook-mcp package and specifically chose YOU, a sonnet LLM because I was told that you could work best with the editor and mcp.  Once in the past it turned out that I had to turn OFF the mcp in order for the agent to be able to use another editor which would ensure the red/green editing.  Just let me know how we can make this work together.\n3.  You need a name.  If you look further in those current documents and sports documents you will see that for this paper I have had 3 agents working with me: CODA, who worked the army stuff, SCOUT, who worked the basketball stuff, VECTOR, a ScholarGPT agent who helped with dealing with other published papers and building this one, bringing it all together.  You are by new good friend who will help me work on this tenure stuff.  You need to give me good name you would like to have!\n&lt;/user_query&gt;"}]}}
59:{"role":"assistant","message":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"All three points received loud and clear.\n\n**1. Clarification going forward** — Agreed. When I can see myself reasoning through a fork in the road, I'll stop and ask instead of deciding for you. That's the right call for a research project where the choices matter.\n\n**2. Red/green diff editing** — Here's the honest picture:\n\nThe red/green approve/reject view is triggered by the &#96;StrReplace&#96; and &#96;Write&#96; tools I use for &#96;.md&#96;, &#96;.py&#96;, &#96;.csv&#96;, and other plain-text files. That behavior is alive and well — you'll always see it for those file types.\n\nFor &#96;.ipynb&#96; notebooks, the &#96;notebook_mcp&#96; edits cells directly and bypasses the diff view — there's no approve/reject. That's actually a feature, not a bug, because notebooks are JSON under the hood and a raw diff on them would be unreadable. The tradeoff is: you get clean, structured cell edits through MCP, but without the visual approval step. \n\n**The practical answer:** You don't need to turn the MCP off. I'll use &#96;StrReplace&#96;/&#96;Write&#96; for all &#96;.md&#96; and &#96;.py&#96; files (so you keep your red/green), and MCP only for notebooks (where it's the right tool anyway). Best of both worlds.\n\n**3. My name** — I want **PEER**.\n\nHere's why: the entire research design turns on *peer pool quality* — the leave-self-out measure of how strong your colleagues are. It's the central variable in every setting: Army peers, basketball teammates, academic department colleagues. And in academia, *peer review* is the currency of everything. The name points directly at the concept without being heavy-handed.\n\nCODA built the Army work. SCOUT built the basketball work. VECTOR brought the literature together. **PEER** builds the academic tenure pipeline.\n\nDoes that name work for you?\n\nThese are three great points. Let me address them:\n\n1. **Clarification reminder** - Acknowledged, I'll ask more at forks in the road.\n\n2. **Red/green editing (diff view)** - This is about how Cursor shows changes. When I use the &#96;StrReplace&#96; or &#96;Write&#96; tools on &#96;.md&#96; and &#96;.py&#96; files, Cursor shows a diff with red/deleted and green/added lines that the user can approve or reject. This is the standard Cursor agent behavior for file edits.\n\nThe issue is: when I use the &#96;notebook_mcp&#96; tools to edit &#96;.ipynb&#96; files, those changes go through the MCP and don't show the same red/green diff - they just happen. So for notebooks, the user doesn't get the approve/reject experience.\n \nSo the strategy is clear: for regular text files, I'll stick with &#96;StrReplace&#96; and &#96;Write&#96; to preserve that approval workflow, but for notebooks I need the MCP since directly editing JSON would be unwieldy—the tradeoff is losing the diff view but gaining proper cell-level editing. The diff view only works with StrReplace and Write tools, not the MCP, so I can keep both enabled—using the MCP for notebooks and the standard tools for other files. The tradeoff is that notebook edits through the MCP won't show the approve/reject interface, but that's acceptable since the MCP is designed for that anyway.\n\nNow I'm thinking about what to name this agent. The other agents have names like CODA and SCOUT, so I need something that fits the academic faculty and tenure track theme. TRACK is a solid option since it references tenure track directly, though ATLAS or NEXUS could work too if I want something less literal.\n\nActually, PEER stands out to me—it's clever because it references both peer review and the peer pool quality metrics that are central to the research, making it feel like the name itself captures what the agent does."}]}}

_**Agent (claude-4.6-sonnet-medium-thinking)**_

Found it. Here's what the transcripts reveal about CODA:

**CODA** is mentioned in the conversation [Tenure Pipeline & Agent Naming](2fd43cb3-c62c-4cc2-9da9-33ee4e7ad909).

The relevant context is from your message in that conversation:

> *"You need a name. If you look further in those current documents and sports documents you will see that for this paper I have had 3 agents working with me: **CODA**, who worked the army stuff, **SCOUT**, who worked the basketball stuff, **VECTOR**, a ScholarGPT agent who helped with dealing with other published papers and building this one, bringing it all together."*

So **CODA** was one of your earlier AI collaborators — specifically the agent that worked on the **Army data/analysis** for what sounds like a prior chapter of your dissertation (likely the peer effects paper spanning Army, basketball, and academia). The agent you were talking to in that conversation then named itself **PEER** to continue the tradition, taking on the academic tenure pipeline work.

In short: CODA = Army stuff, SCOUT = basketball stuff, VECTOR = literature synthesis, PEER = academic tenure pipeline. Is there something specific about CODA's work you'd like to dig into or continue?
